danger_signals.visual_form: enemy units displayed on tactical map grid
danger_signals.meaning: immediate threat to player forces; distance determines engagement risk

danger_signals.visual_form: terrain type visual representation affecting unit positioning
danger_signals.meaning: certain terrain reduces unit mobility, attack power, or defense for terrestrial units

danger_signals.visual_form: unit status at resupply buildings or supply trucks
danger_signals.meaning: aircraft fuel/ammo depletion, infantry transport depletion; units become vulnerable without resupply

danger_signals.visual_form: enemy units positioned adjacent to player headquarters on map
danger_signals.meaning: immediate loss condition threatened; primary objective under direct attack

safety_signals.visual_form: cluster of player-controlled units on tactical map
safety_signals.meaning: strength in numbers; mutual defensive positions reduce individual unit vulnerability

safety_signals.visual_form: airports and supply buildings occupied by player units
safety_signals.meaning: ability to restore aircraft fuel/ammo and replenish infantry; sustained combat capability

safety_signals.visual_form: favorable terrain type occupied by player units
safety_signals.meaning: defensive positioning that improves mobility, attack, or defense for terrestrial forces

safety_signals.visual_form: diverse unit types visible on map (infantry, aircraft, armor, transport)
safety_signals.meaning: rock-paper-scissors coverage; strong matchups available against expected enemy types

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: deliberately obscured in NA release; no named faction labels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: occupied headquarters and controlled territories on strategic map

faction_indicators.faction: Axis Powers (Afrika Korps in Japanese version)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: CPU-controlled units visually distinct from player units on grid
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: opponent headquarters location; initial territory control on map

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: playable faction with distinct unit set; explicitly named as Erwin Rommel's command
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Egyptian campaign theater; desert terrain as primary battle environment

faction_indicators.faction: British Forces (Bernard Montgomery in Japanese version)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: explicitly named opponent in Japanese campaign; enemy faction in Japanese release
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: North African theater; Egypt defense scenario

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: player-controlled units outnumber or match enemy forces on map display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player can execute offensive maneuvers; CPU opponent plays defensively with fixed AI

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: CPU forces outnumber player units (scenario 2-5 difficulty scaling)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player must employ strategic positioning and terrain use to compensate; difficulty increases only through unit quantity, not AI variance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: no time limit imposed; map control divided between factions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player determines pace of engagement; attrition becomes primary victory path

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: headquarters location captured by opposing force on tactical grid
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: immediate loss condition triggered; alternative: complete destruction of all enemy units required

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: terrain type variation displayed on map (affects movement range visualization)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: unit movement paths shortened in difficult terrain; unit attack/defense values adjusted by terrain type on battle resolution

environmental_storytelling.example: North African/Egyptian desert campaign map layout communicates specific WWII theater (1940s conflict context)
airports and supply buildings as interactive map features signal logistics-dependent warfare; resupply mechanic reinforces supply chain vulnerability theme
desert terrain variations visually convey why North Africa was strategically significant; movement/defense penalties tell story of environmental challenge to mechanized warfare
nine distinct unit types (aircraft, armor, infantry, transport) visually represent WWII combined arms doctrine; rock-paper-scissors matchups communicate historical doctrine complexity
headquarters placement and initial unit distribution on map establish faction territorial claims and reinforce historical campaign narrative (Axis invasion, Allied defense)

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: must return to airport facility for resupply; cannot engage without visit to resupply building
can be transported by transport units; affected by terrain for movement range
serves dual role as strategic objective and combat unit; occupancy by enemy triggers loss condition
receives larger unit allocation than player; fixed AI variance (no scaling complexity), only quantitative advantage changes
modifies unit mobility range, attack power, and defense rating during battle sequences (terrestrial units only; aircraft exempt)
provides mobile resupply alternative to fixed facilities; enables forward unit replenishment without returning to infrastructure

notes: Desert Commander uses visual minimalism characteristic of NES strategy games. Faction identity was deliberately obfuscated in North American release (conflict remained opaque; factions unnamed) versus Japanese version which explicitly named Allied/Axis commanders (Patton/Montgomery vs. Rommel). World-state communication relies heavily on: (1) unit positioning on grid, (2) terrain type visual variation, (3) map control/headquarters occupation. Danger/safety signals are primarily spatial and resource-based rather than aesthetic. Political situation (WWII North African campaign) communicated through setting and historical unit types rather than explicit narrative framing in NA version.
